title: Deficiency Symmetries of Surgeries in S3
overall_summary: The paper proves a symmetry theorem comparing the Heegaard Floer correction-term deficiencies of rational negative surgery S^3_{-p/q}(C) on a knot C with those of a related integral negative surgery S^3_{-n}(C), where p=nq-r. It then uses this symmetry, Ozsvath-Szabo's characteristic-covector calculus for negative-definite linear plumbings, and Donaldson diagonalisation to prove a rational-surgery changemaker theorem generalising several results of Greene. The most relevant material for a purely lattice-theoretic problem is not the knot Floer input itself, but the combinatorial machinery: push-down operations on weighted linear graphs, classification of maximising characteristic covectors by no-full-tank conditions, and the way sharpness plus Donaldson forces plumbing rows in a lattice embedding to have entries 0, ±1 with controlled overlaps. The proof of the changemaker theorem also contains reusable row-vector arguments in which the existence of many ±1 characteristic covectors forces strong constraints on supports and subset-sum, changemaker structure.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a knot in S^3 and let p,q>0 be coprime. Write p=nq-r with 0<r<q. For a Spin^c structure t on S^3_{-p/q}(C), define the deficiency D_C^{p/q}(t)=d(S^3_{-p/q}(C),t)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t), using the standard identification with the corresponding surgery on the unknot U; define D_C^n analogously for -n surgery. Then there is a function r: Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)) -> Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)) such that D_C^{p/q}(t)=D_C^n(r(t)) for every t. All fibres of r have size q except one exceptional fibre, lying over a Spin^c structure on S^3_{-n}(C) that minimises D_C^n; in the construction this exceptional fibre has size q-r. Consequently conjugation on Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)), under which D_C^n is invariant, lifts to a function on Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)) under which D_C^{p/q} is invariant.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two independent ingredients whose equality forces the map. First, using the Ni-Wu formula for rational-surgery deficiencies in terms of the monotone sequences V_i and H_i from knot Floer homology, the paper proves the exact sum identity sum_{Spin^c rational} D_C^{p/q} = q sum_{Spin^c integral} D_C^n - r min D_C^n. Second, the paper analyses the negative-definite linear plumbing W' for S^3_{-p/q}(U) with continued fraction p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell], a_1=n. Ozsvath-Szabo's algorithm gives a set K of maximising characteristic covectors with no full tanks. This set is modified to a set K' by pushing down those K whose restriction to G-v_1 is left-full; each push-down preserves the square and hence remains a maximiser. For each representative K' one obtains, from the factorisation S^3 -> S^3_{-n}(C) -> S^3_{-p/q}(C) and the negative-definite cobordism inequality, an inequality D_C^n(v_{K'}) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_{K'}), where v_{K'} is determined by the first coordinate of K' and w_{K'} is the rational Spin^c structure. Counting first coordinates in K' gives q representatives over each integral Spin^c structure except the central minimising one, where there are q-r. Summing all these inequalities gives the same right-hand side as the Ni-Wu sum identity, so every individual inequality is equality. The desired map r sends the rational Spin^c structure represented by K' to the integral Spin^c structure determined by its first coordinate.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y=S^3_{-p/q}(C) for a knot C in S^3 and coprime p,q>0. Write p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell] in Hirzebruch-Jung form with a_i>=2 for i>=2, and set n=a_1. Let W be the trace of the corresponding negative-definite integral surgery diagram with linear chain weights -a_i, where the first component is tied to C as in Figure 1 of the paper. Suppose that -Y bounds a sharp, simply connected, negative-definite smooth 4-manifold X, that H_2(X) is free, and that Q_X is its intersection form. If the deficiency d(Y,i)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),i) vanishes for one Spin^c structure i when n is odd, or for q-r+1 Spin^c structures when n is even in the non-integral notation p=nq-r, then there exists an integral matrix A such that -A A^t = Q_X direct_sum Q_W. If q != 1, A can be chosen so that its last ell rows, corresponding to the W summand, have the staircase changemaker form displayed in the paper: the first of these rows is of the form (sigma_s,...,sigma_1,1,0,...,0), the rows corresponding to vertices v_i for i=2,...,ell have exactly a_i nonzero entries, all equal to ±1, adjacent such rows overlap in exactly one column with opposite signs, non-adjacent such rows do not overlap, and row v_2 overlaps the first row in the single displayed 1-column with opposite sign. The nonnegative integers sigma_1,...,sigma_s form a changemaker set, meaning every integer from 0 to their total sum is a subset sum. If q=1, the last row can be chosen in the form (sigma'_s,...,sigma'_1,sigma'_0), where sigma'_0,...,sigma'_s form a changemaker set.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof begins by applying the gluing and Donaldson setup summarised in Proposition 4.2. Since X and W glue to a closed negative-definite 4-manifold, Donaldson identifies its intersection lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice, yielding an integral matrix A with -A A^t=Q_X direct_sum Q_W. Let the last ell rows of A be x,y_2,...,y_ell, corresponding to the vertices v_1,v_2,...,v_ell of the surgery chain. Lemma 4.3 says that whenever K is a maximising characteristic covector for a Spin^c structure with zero deficiency, K is obtained by pairing one of the rows with a vector alpha all of whose entries are ±1. For i>=2 the paper chooses explicit maximising covectors K with <K,v_i>=a_i and first coordinate among the values guaranteed by Corollary 4.1 to have zero deficiency. Pairing with alpha gives -sum_j alpha_j y_{i,j}=a_i=sum_j y_{i,j}^2, and each summand y_{i,j}^2+alpha_j y_{i,j} is nonnegative; hence every entry y_{i,j} is 0 or ±1, with exactly a_i nonzero entries. To control overlaps, the paper chooses further maximisers with prescribed coordinates at two vertices. For non-adjacent rows y_i and y_j, a maximiser with large values at both forces an alpha that is -1 on the support of y_i and also realises the maximum for y_j; using y_i dot y_j=0 then shows their supports are disjoint. For adjacent rows, a maximiser with coordinates a_i and a_{i+1}-2 forces exactly one shared support column, and the entries there have opposite signs because y_i dot y_{i+1}=-1 in the positive Euclidean row convention. The first row x is then analysed by comparing maximal possible first coordinates among families of zero-deficiency maximisers with <K,v_i>=a_i, a_i-2, or -a_i. For i>=3 these comparisons, together with x dot y_i=0, force x to vanish on the support of y_i. For i=2 they force x to have exactly one nonzero entry on the support of y_2, equal to -1 after signs, giving the displayed single overlap. Finally, the possible first coordinates for zero-deficiency maximisers with <K,v_2>=-a_2 form an entire parity interval. Writing these coordinates as 1+sum sigma_i - 2 sum chi_i sigma_i, with chi_i in {0,1}, shows that the subset sums of the sigma_i realise every integer between 0 and sum sigma_i; this is exactly the changemaker condition. The cases 0<p<q and q=1 are handled by simplifying the same argument: for n=1 all relevant rational deficiencies vanish once one does, and when q=1 there are no rows y_i, so only the changemaker argument for x remains.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.1 and Proposition 2.2 (Ni-Wu formula as used in the paper)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a knot C in S^3, the knot Floer complex gives nonnegative integer sequences V_i and H_i associated to the maps v_i^+ and h_i^+. These satisfy V_0=H_0, the sequence V_i is non-increasing in i, and the sequence H_i is non-decreasing in i. For coprime p,q>0 and for the standard labelling t_i of Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C)), the deficiency is D_C^{p/q}(t_i)=2 max{ V_floor(i/q), H_floor((i-p)/q) }.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The monotonicity and equality V_0=H_0 are quoted from Ni and Wu, as is the deficiency formula. In this paper these facts are used as a finite combinatorial formula: after writing rational Spin^c labels as t_{iq+j}, the floor functions split the sum over rational Spin^c structures into q-r copies of the integral expression and r shifted copies. The monotonicity of V_i and H_i is then the only property needed to identify the contribution of the shifted copies.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let C be a knot in S^3 and let p,q>0 be coprime with p=nq-r and 0<r<q. Then sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C))} D_C^{p/q}(t) = q sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t) - r min_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For integral -n surgery, Proposition 2.2 gives sum D_C^n = 2 sum_{i=0}^{n-1} max{V_i,H_{i-n}}. For rational -p/q surgery, write labels as t_{iq+j}, with 0<=j<q and 0<=i<=n-1-delta(j), where delta(j)=floor((j+r)/q). Thus delta(j)=0 for q-r values of j and delta(j)=1 for r values. The delta=0 terms exactly reproduce the integral sum. For delta=1 one must compare sum_{i=0}^{n-2} max{V_i,H_{i-n+1}} with the integral sum. Choose the largest index i' at which max{V_i,H_{i-n}} is minimal, and split into the two cases V_{i'}>=H_{i'-n} and H_{i'-n}>=V_{i'}. Monotonicity of V and H shows that the shifted sum equals the integral max-sum minus m, where 2m is the minimal integral deficiency. Multiplying by 2 and accounting for the r shifted j-values gives the formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For integral -n surgery on a knot C, using the standard labels t_i on Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C)), if n is even then t_{n/2} realises the minimal deficiency D_C^n, while if n is odd then t_{(n-1)/2} and t_{(n+1)/2} realise the minimal deficiency.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The label t_i evaluates modulo 2n as n+2i, so conjugation sends t_i to t_{n-i}; deficiencies are invariant under conjugation. If t_i is a minimiser with i not equal to 0, then its conjugate t_{n-i} is also a minimiser. Assuming i<=n-i, monotonicity of V and H implies every t_j with i<=j<=n-i also has deficiency at most the same minimum, hence equal to it; therefore the central label or labels are minimisers. If t_0 is a minimiser, then D_C^n(t_0)=2 max{V_0,H_{-n}}=2V_0 because V_0=H_0>=H_{-n}. Since V_i<=V_0 and H_{i-n}<=H_0=V_0, t_0 actually has maximal deficiency; thus if it is also minimal, all deficiencies are equal and the central labels are again minimisers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let G be the negative-definite linear graph with vertices v_1,...,v_ell, weights w(v_i)=-a_i, and p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell] with a_i>=2 for i>=2. A characteristic covector K has coordinates k_i=<K,v_i> congruent to -a_i modulo 2. Suppose K satisfies -a_i+2 <= k_i <= a_i for every i. A vertex v_i is a peak if k_i=a_i. K has a full tank if there exist peaks v_i and v_j with i<j and k_m=a_m-2 for all i<m<j. Then the Spin^c structures on S^3_{-p/q}(U), equivalently on the boundary of the plumbing, are represented by exactly those such K with no full tanks; the paper denotes this collection by K.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Ozsvath-Szabo's algorithm says that characteristic covectors satisfying the initial inequalities represent ker U in HF^+ precisely when they initiate maximising paths under push-downs. Pushing down at a peak v_i means replacing K by K+2 PD[v_i], which changes the i-coordinate from a_i to -a_i and increases adjacent coordinates by 2. If K has a full tank between peaks v_i and v_j, pushing down successively from v_i toward v_j produces an overshoot at v_j, ending with coordinate a_j+2; hence the path is non-maximising. Conversely, assume K has no full tank. The proof inducts on the number of peaks appearing in K and its push-downs. If there is no peak, the path is already terminal and maximising. If there is a peak at v_i, push it down. If neither neighbour becomes a peak, the number of peaks decreases and no full tank is created. If a peak propagates to one side, continue pushing in that direction; the absence of full tanks guarantees this propagation stops before overshooting. If peaks propagate in both directions, push both ways, repeating as needed when a_i=2, until the number of peaks drops. The induction then gives a maximising path. Since the boundary lens space is an L-space, these maximising covectors give all Spin^c structures without repetition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With G, Q, W' and K as above, where Q is the weighted adjacency matrix of the negative-definite plumbing W' with boundary S^3_{-p/q}(U), each Spin^c structure t on S^3_{-p/q}(U) has a unique representative K in the no-full-tank set K such that [K]=t, and this K is a maximiser for t, meaning K Q^{-1} K^t is maximal among characteristic covectors restricting to t. Moreover d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t) = (K Q^{-1} K^t + b_2(W'))/4.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Lemma 3.3 identifies the no-full-tank initial covectors with Ozsvath-Szabo's maximising representatives for ker U. Because S^3_{-p/q}(U) is a lens space and hence an L-space, ker U contains one generator for each Spin^c structure, so there is no repetition. The correction-term formula is the standard Ozsvath-Szabo formula for a sharp negative-definite plumbing: for each boundary Spin^c structure, the d-invariant is the maximum of (K^2+b_2)/4 over extensions, and the representative K in K attains that maximum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be the no-full-tank set of characteristic covectors for the linear plumbing associated to p/q=[a_1,...,a_ell], and write a_1=n and p=nq-r with 0<r<q. Restrict a covector K in K to its first coordinate <K,v_1>. If this first coordinate is not n, then for each allowed first-coordinate value the restriction map has exactly q preimages in K. If the first coordinate is n, then there are exactly q-r elements of K with that first coordinate.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Fix a first coordinate not equal to n. The remaining coordinates must form a characteristic covector on the subchain G-v_1 satisfying the same initial inequalities, initiating a maximising path, and having no full tanks. By Lemma 3.3 this number is the number of Spin^c structures on the lens space corresponding to the continued fraction [a_2,...,a_ell]=q/r, namely q. There are n-1 first-coordinate values other than n, accounting for (n-1)q elements. Since the total number of elements of K is |Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(U))|=p=nq-r, the remaining first-coordinate-n elements number q-r.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.6
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the rational-surgery setup, let F be any complete set of representatives of the equivalence classes of characteristic covectors for the plumbing graph G, and assume each K_i in F is a maximiser for its boundary Spin^c structure. Let s_i be the Spin^c structure on the full trace W(C) with c1(s_i)=K_i. Let w_i=s_i restricted to S^3_{-p/q}(C), and let v_i=s_i restricted to S^3_{-n}(C) through the natural factorisation of the trace S^3 -> S^3_{-n}(C) -> S^3_{-p/q}(C). Then 0 <= D_C^n(v_i) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_i) for every i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Ozsvath-Szabo inequality for a negative-definite cobordism W from Y_1 to Y_2 gives (c1(s)^2+b_2(W))/4 <= d(Y_2,s|Y_2)-d(Y_1,s|Y_1). Apply this first to the second-stage cobordism W_2(C) from S^3_{-n}(C) to S^3_{-p/q}(C). For the unknot cobordism W_2(U), equality holds for the corresponding maximiser by the correction-term calculation for the sharp plumbing; since the intersection form is independent of C, subtracting the unknot equality from the inequality for C gives D_C^n(v_i) <= D_C^{p/q}(w_i). Applying the same inequality to the first-stage cobordism W_1(C) from S^3 to S^3_{-n}(C), and comparing with the unknot case, gives 0 <= D_C^n(v_i).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.7 with Corollaries 3.8 and 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let K be a characteristic covector for the negative-definite linear graph G, not necessarily in the no-full-tank set. If <K,v_i>=a_i, so v_i is a peak, and K'=K+2 PD[v_i] is the push-down at v_i, then (K')^2=K^2, where square is computed using Q^{-1}. Consequently, any push-down of a maximising representative K in K is also a maximiser for the same boundary Spin^c structure, and the set M of all maximisers is closed under push-downs.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: In the basis dual to the vertices, PD[v_i] is the i-th row of the intersection matrix Q, so PD[v_i] Q^{-1}=e_i. Therefore (K+2 PD[v_i]) Q^{-1} (K+2 PD[v_i])^t = K Q^{-1} K^t + 4 e_i K^t + 4 e_i PD[v_i]^t. Here e_i K^t=<K,v_i>=a_i, and e_i PD[v_i]^t=Q_{ii}=-a_i. The two extra terms cancel, giving equal squares. Since push-down does not change the boundary Spin^c class and preserves square, it preserves the property of maximising that square within the class.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.10
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For any knot C in S^3 and p=nq-r with 0<r<q, the deficiencies satisfy sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-p/q}(C))} D_C^{p/q}(t) >= q sum_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t) - r min_{t in Spin^c(S^3_{-n}(C))} D_C^n(t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof constructs a new complete family K' of maximising characteristic covectors from the no-full-tank family K. If K in K has first coordinate j with -1<=j<n and the restriction of K to G-v_1 is left-full, let v_k be the first peak in that restriction and replace K by K+2 sum_{i=1}^k PD[v_i]. This push-down is a maximiser by Lemma 3.7 and increases the first coordinate from j to j+2. All other K are left unchanged. For a fixed first-coordinate value j, exactly r elements are nudged upward: by Lemma 3.5 there are q restrictions on G-v_1 and q-r of them are not left-full when the first coordinate is n, so the left-full count is r. Thus K' has q elements over each integral first-coordinate value except the central value, namely -1 when n is odd or 0 when n is even, where it has q-r. Applying Lemma 3.6 to every member of K' and summing gives the stated inequality; Lemma 2.4 identifies the exceptional central value as one with minimal integral deficiency.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: With the notation used to prove Theorem 1.1, let t be a Spin^c structure on S^3_{-p/q}(C), let K be a maximiser representing t, and let K' be the corresponding representative in the adjusted maximiser family K'. Then D_C^{p/q}(t) is minimal if the first coordinate <K',v_1> is 0 or ±1. If n is even and there are q-r+1 choices of t at which D_C^{p/q} is minimal, this minimality also extends to representatives with first coordinate ±2. In either case, if D_C^{p/q}(t)=0 for at least one t, then the minimal deficiency is zero.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Theorem 1.1 identifies D_C^{p/q}(t) with the integral deficiency D_C^n of the Spin^c structure determined by the first coordinate of K'. Lemma 2.4 says the central integral Spin^c structures, represented by first coordinates 0 and ±1 according to parity conventions, minimise D_C^n. When n is even, the first-coordinate-0 fibre has only q-r representatives, so having q-r+1 rational minima forces at least one additional adjacent fibre to be minimal; the same monotonicity argument used in Lemma 2.4 shows that first coordinates ±2 are the next possible ones. The final assertion uses the non-negativity 0<=D_C^n from Lemma 3.6.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Y be a closed 3-manifold obtained by integral surgery on an ell-component link L with negative-definite linking matrix Q and trace W. Let Y' be obtained by integral surgery on a possibly different link L' with the same linking matrix Q, and suppose the trace W' is sharp. Let X be a sharp negative-definite smooth 4-manifold with boundary -Y, with H_1(X)=0 and H_2(X) free. For a fixed t in Spin^c(Y), there is a bijection between Spin^c structures s on X union_Y W restricting to t and pairs (s_X,s_W) in Spin^c(X) x Spin^c(W) with both restrictions equal to t, and under this bijection c1(s)^2=c1(s_X)^2+c1(s_W)^2. Moreover max_{s|Y=t} c1(s)^2 + b_2(X union_Y W) = 4 d(Y',t) - 4 d(Y,t).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This proposition is quoted as a standard algebraic package from Greene's work. The bijection is the natural gluing statement for Spin^c structures, using that c1 commutes with restriction maps and that the relevant restriction maps are surjective; the paper notes that an odd-determinant hypothesis used elsewhere is unnecessary because H_1(X) and H_1(W) are torsion-free. The square identity follows from the orthogonal splitting of the intersection form under gluing. The max formula comes from sharpness of X and W' together with the Ozsvath-Szabo correction-term inequality: W and W' have the same intersection form, so the maximisation over the glued closed manifold measures exactly the difference between the d-invariants of Y' and Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 4.3
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the setting of Proposition 4.2, additionally in the situation where X union_Y W is a closed simply connected negative-definite 4-manifold so Donaldson's theorem identifies its lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice. Let u_1,...,u_ell be the basis of H_2(W) coming from the surgery link, with images in H_2(X union_Y W). If K is a maximising characteristic covector for a boundary Spin^c structure t=[K] and d(Y',t)-d(Y,t)=0, then there exists a vector alpha in {±1}^{b_2(X)+ell} such that K=(<alpha,u_1>,...,<alpha,u_ell>) in the basis dual to u_1,...,u_ell.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Proposition 4.2 and the zero-deficiency assumption, there is a Spin^c structure s' on X union_Y W with c1(s')^2=-b_2(X union_Y W). Decompose s' into its restrictions to X and W. Since X is sharp and K is a maximiser over the same boundary Spin^c structure, replace the W-component of s' by a Spin^c structure s_W with c1(s_W)=K without changing the total square; the resulting closed-manifold characteristic covector alpha still satisfies alpha^2=-b_2. Donaldson diagonalises the closed negative-definite lattice as (Z^N,-id). A characteristic covector in this lattice has all entries odd, and the equality alpha^2=-N forces every entry to have absolute value 1. The restriction of alpha to H_2(W), computed by pairing alpha with the images of u_i, is precisely K.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: In the special case q=2, so p/q=[n,2], the conclusion of Theorem 1.2 holds under a weaker even-n vanishing assumption: if n is even, it is enough that d(Y,t)-d(S^3_{-p/q}(U),t)=0 for one Spin^c structure t.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For q=2 the second plumbing row y can be put in the form (1,1,0,...,0). The relation x dot y=-1 gives x_1+x_2=-1; choose signs and ordering with x_2<=x_1, which implies x_1>=0. Let S be the set of maximisers K with <K,v_2>=0 and zero deficiency, and let j_max be the maximum first coordinate among them. The assumed single zero deficiency gives j_max>=0. If j_max>0 then enough adjacent first-coordinate values occur to satisfy the original q-r+1 condition, so Theorem 1.2 applies. If j_max=0, the same row-maximisation argument used in the proof of Theorem 1.2 gives j_max=x_1-x_2+sum_{i>=3}|x_i|. Substituting x_2=-1-x_1 yields 0=2x_1+1+sum_{i>=3}|x_i|, impossible because all terms on the right are nonnegative and 1 is positive. Therefore j_max>0 and the theorem applies.

proof_techniques.name: Push-down calculus for characteristic covectors
proof_techniques.description: For a negative-definite plumbing graph, a characteristic covector K can be modified by pushing down at a peak v_i, replacing K by K+2 PD[v_i]. In the linear graph used in the paper, this changes a coordinate equal to a_i into -a_i and increases adjacent coordinates by 2. The paper uses this as a rewriting system: starting from covectors in the initial Ozsvath-Szabo range, paths of push-downs either terminate in the final allowed range, giving maximisers, or overshoot, giving non-maximisers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is a statement about a definite tree lattice. A sign-reversed version of this push-down operation is a natural way to move along vertices of a tree while preserving or controlling a quadratic quantity, so it may help build normal forms or detect configurations forced by the inequality w(v)<d(v).

proof_techniques.name: No-full-tank criterion
proof_techniques.description: In the linear plumbing, a full tank is a pair of peaks with every intermediate coordinate as high as possible without being a peak. The paper proves that full tanks are exactly the local obstruction, within the initial range, to initiating a maximising push-down path: a full tank propagates a push-down into an overshoot, while absence of full tanks permits an induction on peaks.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Although the original tree is not necessarily linear, this suggests looking for path obstructions between large pairings on vertices. A proof by contradiction that every vertex is reducible might produce saturated paths analogous to full tanks, which could then be ruled out by positive definiteness.

proof_techniques.name: Square-preserving peak push-down
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.7 is a purely algebraic computation: if a characteristic covector K pairs with a vertex v_i as <K,v_i>=a_i, then K+2 PD[v_i] has the same square because the linear and quadratic correction terms cancel. This lets the paper move among many representatives of the same boundary Spin^c structure without losing the maximising property.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a graph lattice, decomposability or irreducibility questions often involve comparing norms and pairings after adding multiples of vertices. The cancellation identity gives a template for finding transformations that preserve extremality and may expose forced irreducible vertices.

proof_techniques.name: Counting representatives by deleting a vertex
proof_techniques.description: Lemma 3.5 counts maximising covectors with a fixed first coordinate by identifying the remaining choices with maximising covectors on the subchain G-v_1. Since the subchain represents the continued fraction q/r, the count is q for ordinary first-coordinate values; the exceptional value is then obtained by subtracting from the total p.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For a general weighted tree, deleting a vertex and comparing the lattice or determinant of the remaining forest could be a useful inductive counting device. The hypothesis w(v)<d(v) singles out a vertex where such deletion-and-counting arguments might become imbalanced.

proof_techniques.name: Summing inequalities until equality forces pointwise equality
proof_techniques.description: The proof of Theorem 1.1 obtains individual inequalities D_C^n(v_i)<=D_C^{p/q}(w_i) from cobordism theory, then sums them over a carefully chosen family of maximisers. A separate exact formula for the total sum shows the summed inequality is actually an equality, forcing every individual inequality in the family to be equality.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: In a lattice-only proof, one might set up local inequalities associated to putative decompositions of vertices and then sum over all vertices or over a normal-form family. If positive definiteness gives an exact global identity, equality forcing could isolate a vertex where decomposition is impossible.

proof_techniques.name: Donaldson diagonalisation plus zero deficiency gives ±1 test vectors
proof_techniques.description: After gluing a sharp negative-definite filling X to a surgery trace W, Donaldson's theorem identifies the closed intersection lattice with the standard negative diagonal lattice. If a boundary Spin^c structure has zero deficiency, the corresponding characteristic covector in the closed lattice has square -N; since characteristic covectors in -I have odd entries, all entries must be ±1. Pairing this ±1 vector with the W rows realises the desired maximising characteristic covector.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original tree lattice can be embedded into a standard definite lattice, or if one can otherwise produce enough sign vectors testing the rows, the paper's argument shows how ±1 pairings can force strong restrictions on vertex vectors. This is especially relevant for proving that some vertex cannot be decomposed with nonnegative cross-pairing.

proof_techniques.name: Row-support forcing from ±1 pairings
proof_techniques.description: In the proof of Theorem 1.2, a row y_i with norm a_i is tested against a sign vector alpha satisfying -<alpha,y_i>=a_i. Since a_i=sum y_{i,j}^2, the equality sum_j(y_{i,j}^2+alpha_j y_{i,j})=0 and nonnegativity of each summand force y_{i,j} in {0,±1}. Similar tests with two rows force non-adjacent rows to have disjoint supports and adjacent rows to overlap exactly once with opposite signs.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is one of the most directly portable lattice arguments. If vertices of the tree are represented as vectors in a standard positive or negative lattice, finding sign vectors that maximise pairings can force vertices to be sums of unit coordinate vectors with controlled overlaps, a structure that may make irreducibility of at least one vertex transparent.

proof_techniques.name: Changemaker extraction from a full interval of pairings
proof_techniques.description: Once the first row x has been shown to have a special form with entries sigma_i and one unit overlap, the paper considers all possible first coordinates j of zero-deficiency maximisers. These j form a complete parity interval and can be written as j=1+sum sigma_i-2 sum chi_i sigma_i. Therefore the subset sums sum chi_i sigma_i realise every integer from 0 to sum sigma_i, exactly the changemaker condition.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a proof of the original problem reduces the tree lattice to a standard-coordinate model, interval-realisation of pairings may similarly imply subset-sum constraints. Such constraints can be used to find extremal or indecomposable coordinate-support vertices.

other_useful_info: The paper explicitly notes that in the gluing proposition a determinant-odd assumption used in earlier work is not necessary; torsion-freeness of H_1(X) and H_1(W) suffices for the needed Spin^c restriction surjectivity. The Ozsvath-Szabo plumbed-manifold algorithm used here is cited as [7], Greene's earlier changemaker and lattice-embedding arguments as [2] and [3], and Donaldson diagonalisation as [1]. Section 4.4 remarks that the asymmetry in Theorem 1.2 between odd n and even n might be removable by choosing a different map r, but the paper does not do so; it does prove the q=2 simplification in Proposition 4.4.
why_relevant: Generalizes Greene's changemaker theorems to rational surgeries. It gives lattice-embedding and changemaker machinery that is often used to convert definite plumbing problems into finite combinatorial lattice problems.
